docs.title: Humanoid Robot Fundamentals
docs.text:
This repository contains educational examples for humanoid robotics.

Start with Workspace Setup.

docs.title: Computer Simulation
docs.text:
unzip mujoco_humanoid_workshop.zip cd mujoco_humanoid_workshop

The folder should contain:

README.md requirements.txt common.py exercise_01_inspect.py exercise_02_pose.py exercise_03_contacts.py exercise_04_push_test.py models/

rm -rf .venv python3 -m venv .venv source .venv/bin/activate

python -m pip install --upgrade pip python -m pip install -r requirements.txt

First test without graphics:

python exercise_01_inspect.py --headless --duration 0.1

This should finish with:

Simulation completed at t=0.10 s

Then test the standalone MuJoCo viewer:

python -m mujoco.viewer --mjcf=models/simple_humanoid.xml

Finally run the corrected exercise:

python exercise_01_inspect.py

The essential correction in Exercise 1 is that this unsafe line was removed:

viewer.opt.flags[mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTPOINT] = True

Exercises 3 and 4 now use the required locking pattern:

with viewer.lock(): viewer.opt.flags[mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTPOINT] = True viewer.opt.flags[mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTFORCE] = True

viewer.sync()

I verified the corrected XML and all four scripts in headless mode with MuJoCo 3.10.0. I could not verify the graphical window in this execution environment because it does not provide a usable desktop display.

If the standalone viewer command also segfaults, the workshop code is no longer the likely cause; that indicates an OpenGL/GLFW or mixed-environment problem. MuJoCo provides its interactive viewer through the Python package, while launch_passive uses a separate viewer thread. In that case, run:

python -X faulthandler exercise_01_inspect.py

and avoid mixing Conda-provided GLFW/OpenGL libraries with a pip-installed MuJoCo environment.

can you make a downloadable .md file out of this?

not the segfault fix, the tutorial

Library / ADC Prep /mujoco_humanoid_workshop_tutorial.md

docs.title: 1. Workshop purpose
docs.text:
This workshop introduces the simulation concepts needed for humanoid robotics using MuJoCo.

The workshop focuses entirely on the simulator. It does not use:

  • machine learning;
  • reinforcement learning;
  • Gymnasium;
  • Stable-Baselines3;
  • ROS;
  • trajectory optimization.

The main topics are:

  • MJCF model structure;
  • floating-base humanoids;
  • generalized coordinates;
  • joint actuators;
  • scripted posture changes;
  • contact forces;
  • friction;
  • centre of mass;
  • external disturbances;
  • fall detection.

The workshop uses a simplified humanoid model first. A Unitree G1 model can be shown at the end as a more realistic example.

docs.title: 2. Learning outcomes
docs.text:
At the end of the workshop, students should be able to:

  1. load an MJCF humanoid model;
  2. identify bodies, joints, geoms, sites, actuators, sensors, and keyframes;
  3. explain the difference between MjModel and MjData;
  4. inspect qpos, qvel, and ctrl;
  5. reset a simulation using a keyframe;
  6. command position actuators;
  7. generate a smooth scripted posture;
  8. visualize and measure foot contact;
  9. modify friction parameters;
  10. apply an external force;
  11. define a simple fall criterion;
  12. explain why a valid humanoid pose is not necessarily dynamically stable.

docs.title: 3. Recommended workshop structure
docs.text: Time Activity 0–10 min Introduction and demonstration 10–30 min Exercise 1: Inspect the humanoid 30–55 min Exercise 2: Scripted posture control 55–65 min Discussion and short break 65–90 min Exercise 3: Contact, CoM, and friction 90–115 min Exercise 4: Push test and fall detection 115–120 min Unitree G1 comparison and conclusions

docs.title: 4. Recommended environment
docs.text:
Recommended system:

  • Ubuntu 22.04 or newer;
  • Python 3.10 or newer;
  • working OpenGL desktop environment;
  • one computer per student or student pair.

The workshop requires only:

mujoco
numpy

docs.title: 5. Extract the workshop package
docs.text:
Unzip the workshop archive:

unzip mujoco_humanoid_workshop_fixed.zip
cd mujoco_humanoid_workshop

The folder should contain:

mujoco_humanoid_workshop/
├── README.md
├── requirements.txt
├── common.py
├── exercise_01_inspect.py
├── exercise_02_pose.py
├── exercise_03_contacts.py
├── exercise_04_push_test.py
└── models/
    └── simple_humanoid.xml

docs.title: 6. Create a Python virtual environment
docs.text:
Create the environment:

python3 -m venv .venv

Activate it:

source .venv/bin/activate

Upgrade pip:

python -m pip install --upgrade pip

Install the requirements:

python -m pip install -r requirements.txt

Verify MuJoCo:

python -c "import mujoco; print(mujoco.__version__)"

docs.title: 7. Test the model without graphics
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_01_inspect.py --headless --duration 0.1

Expected final output:

Simulation completed at t=0.10 s

This confirms that:

  • MuJoCo is installed;
  • the XML model compiles;
  • the physics loop works.

docs.title: 8. Test the graphical viewer
docs.text:
Run:

python -m mujoco.viewer --mjcf=models/simple_humanoid.xml

A humanoid model should appear above the floor.

Close the viewer before continuing.

docs.title: 9. MjModel and MjData
docs.text:
A basic MuJoCo program begins with:

import mujoco

model = mujoco.MjModel.from_xml_path(
    "models/simple_humanoid.xml"
)

data = mujoco.MjData(model)

docs.title: MjModel
docs.text:
MjModel contains the compiled model definition.

Examples include:

  • body masses;
  • joint types;
  • actuator gains;
  • geometry dimensions;
  • friction coefficients;
  • sensor definitions;
  • simulation timestep.

The model is mostly constant during a simulation.

docs.title: MjData
docs.text:
MjData contains the changing simulation state.

Examples include:

  • joint positions;
  • joint velocities;
  • actuator commands;
  • body positions;
  • contact information;
  • sensor values;
  • simulation time.

docs.title: 10. The simulation loop
docs.text:
A simple loop is:

while simulation_is_running:
    data.ctrl[:] = control_command
    mujoco.mj_step(model, data)

mj_step:

  1. evaluates forces;
  2. solves contacts and constraints;
  3. calculates accelerations;
  4. integrates the state;
  5. advances simulation time.

The simulation timestep is stored in:

model.opt.timestep

For example:

<option timestep="0.002"
        gravity="0 0 -9.81"/>

A timestep of 0.002 seconds corresponds to:

[ f = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500\ \text{Hz} ]

docs.title: 11. Main MJCF sections
docs.text:
Open:

models/simple_humanoid.xml

The major sections are:

<mujoco>
    <option/>
    <default/>
    <asset/>
    <worldbody/>
    <equality/>
    <actuator/>
    <sensor/>
    <keyframe/>
</mujoco>

docs.title: 12. Bodies
docs.text:
A body represents a rigid coordinate frame in the kinematic tree.

Example:

<body name="pelvis" pos="0 0 0.92">

A child body is positioned relative to its parent.

Example:

<body name="left_shin" pos="0 0 -0.36">

This means the shin frame is positioned 0.36 m below its parent body.

The simplified hierarchy is:

pelvis
├── torso
│   ├── head
│   ├── left_arm
│   └── right_arm
├── left_thigh
│   └── left_shin
│       └── left_foot
└── right_thigh
    └── right_shin
        └── right_foot

docs.title: 13. Joints
docs.text:
A joint defines relative motion between a body and its parent.

Example hinge joint:

<joint name="left_knee"
       type="hinge"
       axis="0 1 0"
       range="0 2.1"/>

This joint:

  • rotates around the local (y)-axis;
  • has a minimum angle of 0 rad;
  • has a maximum angle of 2.1 rad.

Convert the maximum to degrees:

[ 2.1 \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 120.3^\circ ]

docs.title: 14. The floating base
docs.text:
The pelvis has a free joint:

<freejoint name="root"/>

A free joint allows:

  • translation in (x);
  • translation in (y);
  • translation in (z);
  • rotation around all three axes.

A free joint occupies:

  • 7 entries in qpos;
  • 6 entries in qvel.

docs.title: Position representation
docs.text:
The free joint position uses:

  • 3 Cartesian coordinates;
  • 4 quaternion coordinates.

Therefore:

[ n_{q,\mathrm{free}} = 3 + 4 = 7 ]

docs.title: Velocity representation
docs.text:
The free joint velocity uses:

  • 3 linear velocities;
  • 3 angular velocities.

Therefore:

[ n_{v,\mathrm{free}} = 3 + 3 = 6 ]

The teaching humanoid has:

  • one free joint;
  • twelve hinge joints.

Therefore:

[ n_q = 7 + 12 = 19 ]

[ n_v = 6 + 12 = 18 ]

docs.title: 15. Geoms
docs.text:
A geom can provide:

  • visual shape;
  • collision shape;
  • mass;
  • inertia;
  • contact properties.

Example:

<geom name="left_foot_geom"
      type="box"
      size="0.12 0.05 0.03"
      friction="0.9 0.005 0.0001"/>

A box size specifies half-dimensions.

Therefore, this foot has approximate full dimensions:

[ 0.24 \times 0.10 \times 0.06\ \text{m} ]

docs.title: 16. Sites
docs.text:
A site is a reference location or volume.

Sites are often used for:

  • sensors;
  • end-effector markers;
  • contact measurement regions;
  • visualization;
  • force application reference points.

Example:

<site name="left_foot_touch_site"
      type="box"
      size="0.12 0.05 0.015"/>

docs.title: 17. Actuators
docs.text:
The workshop uses position actuators.

Example:

<position name="left_knee_act"
          joint="left_knee"
          kp="140"
          ctrlrange="0 2.1"/>

The command is written to:

data.ctrl

For a position actuator, the control value acts as a desired joint position.

However, the joint is not teleported to that position. The actuator generates force based on the actuator model.

The resulting motion also depends on:

  • inertia;
  • gravity;
  • damping;
  • contact;
  • actuator gain;
  • constraints;
  • timestep.

docs.title: 18. Keyframes
docs.text:
Keyframes store predefined simulation states.

Example:

<keyframe>
    <key name="stand"
         qpos="..."
         ctrl="..."/>

    <key name="crouch"
         qpos="..."
         ctrl="..."/>
</keyframe>

Reset to a keyframe:

key_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_KEY,
    "stand",
)

mujoco.mj_resetDataKeyframe(
    model,
    data,
    key_id,
)

mujoco.mj_forward(model, data)

mj_forward recalculates derived quantities after manually changing the state.

docs.title: 19. Pelvis weld
docs.text:
The model includes an equality constraint:

<equality>
    <weld name="pelvis_weld"
          body1="pelvis"/>
</equality>

This weld can connect the pelvis to the world.

docs.title: Fixed-base mode
docs.text:
Used in Exercises 1 and 2.

The humanoid cannot fall.

This allows students to focus on:

  • model structure;
  • joint coordinates;
  • actuator commands;
  • scripted posture changes.

docs.title: Floating-base mode
docs.text:
Used in Exercises 3 and 4.

The weld is disabled from Python:

weld_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_EQUALITY,
    "pelvis_weld",
)

data.eq_active[weld_id] = 0

The humanoid can then:

  • translate;
  • rotate;
  • slip;
  • tip;
  • fall.

docs.title: Part IV — Exercise 1: Inspect the humanoid
20. Objective
docs.text: Understand how an MJCF description becomes an indexed numerical model.

docs.title: 21. Run the exercise
docs.text:
python exercise_01_inspect.py

The script prints:

  • body count;
  • joint count;
  • number of position coordinates;
  • number of velocity coordinates;
  • actuator count;
  • sensor count;
  • joint names;
  • joint ranges;
  • qpos addresses;
  • degree-of-freedom addresses;
  • actuator names;
  • sensor names.

docs.title: 22. Student task: inspect the model dimensions
docs.text:
Expected values are approximately:

Bodies:               12
Joints:               13
Position coordinates: 19
Velocity coordinates: 18
Actuators:            12
Sensors:              6
Equality constraints: 1

Ask:

  1. Why are there more bodies than actuators?
  2. Why is nq different from nv?
  3. Why does the head have no actuator?
  4. Which joint represents the floating base?

docs.title: 23. Student task: inspect joint names
docs.text:
A typical inspection loop is:

for joint_id in range(model.njnt):
    name = mujoco.mj_id2name(
        model,
        mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_JOINT,
        joint_id,
    )

    print(joint_id, name)

Students should identify:

root
left_hip_yaw
left_hip_roll
left_hip_pitch
left_knee
left_ankle_pitch
left_ankle_roll
right_hip_yaw
right_hip_roll
right_hip_pitch
right_knee
right_ankle_pitch
right_ankle_roll

docs.title: 24. Student task: modify a material
docs.text:
Find:

<material name="left_mat"
          rgba="0.25 0.65 0.35 1"/>

Change it to:

<material name="left_mat"
          rgba="0.8 0.2 0.8 1"/>

Restart:

python exercise_01_inspect.py

The model is compiled when loaded. XML changes do not affect a model that is already running.

docs.title: 25. Student task: modify one joint limit
docs.text:
Find:

<joint name="left_knee"
       range="0 2.1"/>

Change it temporarily:

<joint name="left_knee"
       range="0 1.2"/>

Restart the simulation and observe whether the crouching keyframe or commanded posture remains feasible.

Restore the original value afterward.

docs.title: 26. Exercise 1 discussion
docs.text:
Ask:

  • Which parts are visual only?
  • Which parts participate in collisions?
  • Why does the pelvis need a free joint?
  • Why are joint positions stored in an array?
  • Why are names converted to integer IDs?

docs.title: Main takeaway
docs.text: The XML is a human-readable model description. MuJoCo compiles it into numerical arrays used by the physics engine.

docs.title: Part V — Exercise 2: Scripted posture control
27. Objective
docs.text: Command the humanoid to move smoothly between standing and crouching poses.

docs.title: 28. Run the exercise
docs.text:
python exercise_02_pose.py

The robot should repeatedly move:

standing → crouching → standing

The pelvis is fixed during this exercise.

docs.title: 29. Actuator order
docs.text:
The control vector contains 12 values:

0  left hip yaw
1  left hip roll
2  left hip pitch
3  left knee
4  left ankle pitch
5  left ankle roll
6  right hip yaw
7  right hip roll
8  right hip pitch
9  right knee
10 right ankle pitch
11 right ankle roll

A crouching reference may be:

crouch = [
    0.0, 0.0, -0.45, 0.90, -0.45, 0.0,
    0.0, 0.0, -0.45, 0.90, -0.45, 0.0,
]

For each leg:

[ q_{\mathrm{hip}} + q_{\mathrm{knee}} + q_{\mathrm{ankle}} = -0.45 + 0.90 - 0.45 = 0 ]

This helps keep the foot approximately parallel to the floor.

docs.title: 30. Linear interpolation
docs.text:
The simplest interpolation is:

[ q_{\mathrm{ref}}(u) = q_{\mathrm{start}} + u \left( q_{\mathrm{goal}} - q_{\mathrm{start}} \right) ]

where:

[ 0 \leq u \leq 1 ]

Python:

q_ref = q_start + u * (q_goal - q_start)

This creates a constant desired velocity during the segment, but the desired velocity changes abruptly at the beginning and end.

docs.title: 31. Smoothstep interpolation
docs.text:
The workshop uses:

[ s(u)=3u^2-2u^3 ]

Then:

[ q_{\mathrm{ref}}(u) = q_{\mathrm{start}} + s(u) \left( q_{\mathrm{goal}} - q_{\mathrm{start}} \right) ]

Properties:

[ s(0)=0 ]

[ s(1)=1 ]

[ s'(0)=0 ]

[ s'(1)=0 ]

Python:

def smoothstep(value: float) -> float:
    value = float(np.clip(value, 0.0, 1.0))
    return 3.0 * value**2 - 2.0 * value**3

docs.title: 32. Student task: compare linear and smooth motion
docs.text:
Replace:

return 3.0 * value**2 - 2.0 * value**3

with:

return value

Run:

python exercise_02_pose.py

Compare:

  • movement onset;
  • movement termination;
  • visible oscillation;
  • tracking error;
  • apparent smoothness.

Restore the smoothstep function afterward.

docs.title: 33. Student task: modify actuator gain
docs.text:
Find the position actuator default:

<position kp="140"
          ctrllimited="true"/>

Test:

<position kp="40"
          ctrllimited="true"/>

Then:

<position kp="300"
          ctrllimited="true"/>

Record:

Gain Tracking speed Tracking error Oscillation 40 140 300

docs.title: Expected observations
docs.text:
Low gain:

  • slower response;
  • larger position error;
  • softer movement.

High gain:

  • faster response;
  • smaller static error;
  • possibly stronger oscillation;
  • larger forces;
  • greater numerical sensitivity.

docs.title: 34. Student task: modify joint damping
docs.text:
Find:

<joint damping="3"
       armature="0.02"
       limited="true"/>

Test:

damping="0.3"

Then:

damping="10"

Record the effect.

docs.title: Expected observations
docs.text:
Low damping:

  • more oscillation;
  • longer settling;
  • freer motion.

High damping:

  • reduced oscillation;
  • slower motion;
  • stronger resistance to rapid changes.

docs.title: 35. Exercise 2 discussion
docs.text:
Ask:

  1. Does data.ctrl directly set qpos?
  2. Why does the actual position lag behind the reference?
  3. Why can high gains cause instability?
  4. Why did we fix the pelvis?
  5. Would the same command work if link masses were doubled?

docs.title: Main takeaway
docs.text: An actuator command creates force. It does not directly replace the simulated joint state.

docs.title: Part VI — Discussion and short break
36. Suggested questions
docs.text:
1. Why did we fix the pelvis during the first two exercises?
  2. What changes when the floating base is released?
  3. Can a robot follow all joint commands and still fall?
  4. Is joint-space tracking identical to balance control?
  5. Why can an upright pose be dynamically unstable?

The central idea is:

Good joint tracking does not automatically produce whole-body stability.

docs.title: Part VII — Exercise 3: Contact, centre of mass, and friction
37. Objective
docs.text: Observe floor contact, foot forces, centre of mass, slipping, and load transfer.

docs.title: 38. Run the default experiment
docs.text:
python exercise_03_contacts.py --friction 0.9

The script:

  1. loads the standing keyframe;
  2. disables the pelvis weld;
  3. enables contact visualization;
  4. reads foot contact sensors;
  5. reads the centre-of-mass sensor;
  6. applies a small lateral force;
  7. measures foot displacement.

docs.title: 39. Viewer locking
docs.text:
When using the passive viewer, modify viewer state while holding the viewer lock:

with viewer.lock():
    viewer.opt.flags[
        mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTPOINT
    ] = True

    viewer.opt.flags[
        mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTFORCE
    ] = True

viewer.sync()

This avoids race conditions between the simulation thread and the viewer thread.

docs.title: 40. Foot touch sensors
docs.text:
The XML contains:

<touch name="left_foot_force"
       site="left_foot_touch_site"/>

<touch name="right_foot_force"
       site="right_foot_touch_site"/>

The touch sensor returns a non-negative scalar representing the summed normal contact force associated with contacts in the sensor site.

Read sensor data:

sensor_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_SENSOR,
    "left_foot_force",
)

start = model.sensor_adr[sensor_id]
dimension = model.sensor_dim[sensor_id]

left_force = data.sensordata[
    start:start + dimension
]

docs.title: 41. Centre-of-mass sensor
docs.text:
The XML contains:

<subtreecom name="humanoid_com"
            body="pelvis"/>

The sensor returns:

[ p_{\mathrm{CoM}} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{\mathrm{CoM}}\ y_{\mathrm{CoM}}\ z_{\mathrm{CoM}} \end{bmatrix} ]

For a basic balance interpretation, use the horizontal projection:

[ p_{\mathrm{CoM,xy}} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{\mathrm{CoM}}\ y_{\mathrm{CoM}} \end{bmatrix} ]

For static balance, the projected centre of mass should normally remain inside the support region formed by the contacting feet.

This is only an introductory approximation. Dynamic balance also depends on:

  • velocity;
  • angular momentum;
  • contact forces;
  • centre of pressure;
  • future motion.

docs.title: 42. Student task: compare left and right foot force
docs.text:
Before the disturbance:

left force ≈ right force

During a lateral disturbance:

left force ≠ right force

Ask:

  • Which foot becomes more loaded?
  • Does the foot with greater force necessarily move less?
  • Does force difference directly indicate body velocity?

The answer to the last question is no. Contact force and body velocity are related through dynamics, but they are not the same quantity.

docs.title: 43. Friction parameters
docs.text:
The floor geom contains:

friction="0.9 0.005 0.0001"

The three components correspond to:

  1. sliding friction;
  2. torsional friction;
  3. rolling friction.

For this workshop, the first value is the most important.

docs.title: 44. Student task: friction experiment
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_03_contacts.py --friction 0.9

Then:

python exercise_03_contacts.py --friction 0.3

Then:

python exercise_03_contacts.py --friction 0.05

Record:

Sliding friction Left-foot displacement Right-foot displacement Visible slipping 0.90 0.30 0.05

Expected trend:

  • high friction resists sliding;
  • low friction allows larger foot displacement;
  • very low friction may produce sliding before strong body tilt.

docs.title: 45. Student task: modify the lateral disturbance
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_03_contacts.py \
    --friction 0.3 \
    --lateral-force 10

Then:

python exercise_03_contacts.py \
    --friction 0.3 \
    --lateral-force 40

Observe:

  • load transfer;
  • foot sliding;
  • torso tilt;
  • contact loss;
  • whole-body translation.

docs.title: 46. Contact versus stability
docs.text:
A humanoid can have both feet touching the floor and still be unstable.

Possible situations include:

  • centre of mass leaving the support region;
  • insufficient friction;
  • excessive angular velocity;
  • one foot unloading;
  • contact occurring only at an edge;
  • feet sliding together.

docs.title: Main takeaway
docs.text: Contact is not simply “on” or “off.” It has geometry, forces, friction, and motion.

docs.title: Part VIII — Exercise 4: Push test and fall detection
47. Objective
docs.text: Apply a repeatable external disturbance and measure whether the humanoid falls.

docs.title: 48. Run the push test
docs.text:
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 30

The script applies a horizontal force to the torso.

docs.title: 49. External force array
docs.text:
MuJoCo provides:

data.xfrc_applied

Each body receives a six-component spatial force:

Fx Fy Fz Tx Ty Tz

Apply a force in the world (x)-direction:

torso_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_BODY,
    "torso",
)

data.xfrc_applied[torso_id, 0] = 30.0

Clear the force afterward:

data.xfrc_applied[torso_id, :] = 0.0

docs.title: 50. Push timing
docs.text:
The force is applied only during a defined interval:

if push_start <= data.time < push_start + push_duration:
    data.xfrc_applied[torso_id, 0] = push_force
else:
    data.xfrc_applied[torso_id, :] = 0.0

Typical settings:

push_start    = 0.50 s
push_duration = 0.15 s

docs.title: 51. Force and impulse
docs.text:
Force magnitude alone does not fully describe the disturbance.

Impulse is:

[ J = F \Delta t ]

For a 30 N force applied for 0.15 s:

[ J = 30 \cdot 0.15 = 4.5\ \mathrm{N\,s} ]

For 40 N:

[ J = 40 \cdot 0.15 = 6.0\ \mathrm{N\,s} ]

docs.title: 52. Student task: force sweep
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 20 --headless
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 25 --headless
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 30 --headless
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 35 --headless
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 40 --headless

Record:

Force Impulse Fallen Maximum tilt Minimum pelvis height 20 N 3.00 N·s 25 N 3.75 N·s 30 N 4.50 N·s 35 N 5.25 N·s 40 N 6.00 N·s

docs.title: 53. Fall criterion based on pelvis height
docs.text:
Get the pelvis position:

pelvis_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_BODY,
    "pelvis",
)

pelvis_height = data.xpos[pelvis_id, 2]

Simple criterion:

pelvis_height < 0.55

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• simple;
  • easy to understand;
  • computationally inexpensive.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• a deep crouch may be classified as a fall;
  • a leaning robot may remain above the threshold;
  • body orientation is ignored.

docs.title: 54. Fall criterion based on torso orientation
docs.text:
The body rotation matrix is stored in:

data.xmat[torso_id]

Reshape it:

rotation = data.xmat[torso_id].reshape(3, 3)

The local torso (z)-axis expressed in world coordinates is the third column.

For the workshop model:

torso_up_z = rotation[2, 2]

For an upright torso:

[ z_{\mathrm{up}} \approx 1 ]

For a horizontal torso:

[ z_{\mathrm{up}} \approx 0 ]

A threshold of:

torso_up_z < 0.65

corresponds approximately to:

[ \theta > \cos^{-1}(0.65) \approx 49.5^\circ ]

docs.title: 55. Combined fall criterion
docs.text:
Use:

fallen = (
    pelvis_height < 0.55
    or torso_up_z < 0.65
)

This combines:

  • body height;
  • body orientation.

It is more robust than either condition alone.

docs.title: 56. Student task: change the fall threshold
docs.text:
Try:

torso_up_z < 0.85

This corresponds to:

[ \theta > \cos^{-1}(0.85) \approx 31.8^\circ ]

Then try:

torso_up_z < 0.30

This corresponds to:

[ \theta > \cos^{-1}(0.30) \approx 72.5^\circ ]

Discuss:

  • false positives;
  • false negatives;
  • how the experimental definition changes the reported result.

docs.title: 57. Student task: compare equal impulses
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_04_push_test.py \
    --force 40 \
    --push-duration 0.10 \
    --headless

Impulse:

[ J = 40 \cdot 0.10 = 4.0\ \mathrm{N\,s} ]

Then:

python exercise_04_push_test.py \
    --force 20 \
    --push-duration 0.20 \
    --headless

Impulse:

[ J = 20 \cdot 0.20 = 4.0\ \mathrm{N\,s} ]

Ask:

Will the resulting motion be identical?

Not necessarily.

Even with equal impulse:

  • the force magnitude differs;
  • the body moves during the push;
  • contact conditions change;
  • joint velocities change;
  • the model is nonlinear.

docs.title: 58. Student task: compare friction during the push
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_04_push_test.py \
    --force 30 \
    --friction 0.9 \
    --headless

Then:

python exercise_04_push_test.py \
    --force 30 \
    --friction 0.1 \
    --headless

Determine whether failure occurs primarily through:

  • sliding;
  • tipping;
  • contact loss;
  • a combination of these.

docs.title: 59. Final engineering challenge
docs.text:
Find the largest horizontal push that the humanoid can survive for five seconds.

Students may modify:

  • stance width;
  • knee angle;
  • actuator gain;
  • joint damping;
  • foot friction.

Students may not modify:

  • gravity;
  • body masses;
  • push duration;
  • fall criterion.

Suggested table:

Configuration Maximum force Fell? Maximum tilt Minimum pelvis height Default Wider stance Bent knees Higher damping Final design

docs.title: 60. Purpose
docs.text:
The G1 demonstration shows that the same concepts transfer to a detailed commercial humanoid model.

The detailed model still contains:

  • bodies;
  • joints;
  • geoms;
  • actuators;
  • sensors;
  • contacts;
  • floating-base coordinates.

docs.title: 61. Obtain MuJoCo Menagerie
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone https://github.com/google-deepmind/mujoco_menagerie.git

Open the G1 model:

python -m mujoco.viewer \
    --mjcf mujoco_menagerie/unitree_g1/scene.xml

docs.title: 62. Student observation tasks
docs.text:
Ask students to identify:

  1. pelvis or trunk root;
  2. floating base;
  3. hip joints;
  4. knee joints;
  5. ankle joints;
  6. foot collision geometry;
  7. actuator definitions;
  8. keyframes;
  9. joint limits;
  10. differences from the simplified model.

docs.title: 63. Discussion
docs.text:
Ask:

  • Why is the G1 XML much longer?
  • Which details are physical?
  • Which details are visual?
  • Which concepts are unchanged?
  • Why is a simplified model useful for learning?
  • Why is a detailed model useful for validation?

docs.title: Main takeaway
docs.text: A realistic humanoid is more complex, but it is built from the same simulator concepts.

docs.title: 64. Opening explanation
docs.text: In this workshop, we will not design an intelligent controller. Our goal is to understand what a humanoid simulator actually represents. We will inspect the model, command the joints, release the floating base, observe contact, change friction, and finally apply a controlled push.

docs.title: 65. Before Exercise 1
docs.text: The XML file is the human-readable description. MuJoCo compiles it into indexed arrays. The simulator does not repeatedly search for a joint by name during every dynamics calculation.

docs.title: 66. Before Exercise 2
docs.text: A position actuator does not teleport a joint. It creates force according to the difference between the command and the simulated state. The motion still depends on physics.

docs.title: 67. Before Exercise 3
docs.text: Until now, the pelvis was fixed. This separated joint actuation from whole-body balance. We will now release the root and let the robot interact freely with the floor.

docs.title: 68. Before Exercise 4
docs.text: A push test is only useful if it is repeatable. We must define the force, duration, application point, friction, initial state, and fall criterion.

docs.title: 69. Final conclusion
docs.text: A valid pose is not automatically stable. A joint controller does not directly control the floating base. Humanoid simulation is the interaction of model structure, actuation, contacts, and measurable evaluation criteria.

docs.title: 70. MuJoCo is not installed
docs.text:
Error:

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'mujoco'

Activate the environment:

source .venv/bin/activate

Install:

python -m pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 71. Test without graphics
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_01_inspect.py --headless --duration 0.1

If this works, the model and physics engine are functioning.

docs.title: 72. Viewer segmentation fault
docs.text:
First test the standalone viewer:

python -m mujoco.viewer \
    --mjcf=models/simple_humanoid.xml

If the standalone viewer also crashes, the issue is likely related to:

  • OpenGL;
  • GLFW;
  • graphics drivers;
  • remote desktop;
  • WSL graphics;
  • mixed Conda and pip libraries.

Use a clean virtual environment.

Avoid mixing:

  • Conda OpenGL libraries;
  • system MuJoCo installations;
  • pip MuJoCo installations.

Run with fault reporting:

python -X faulthandler exercise_01_inspect.py

docs.title: 73. Viewer-state changes
docs.text:
When using:

mujoco.viewer.launch_passive(...)

protect viewer changes:

with viewer.lock():
    viewer.opt.flags[
        mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTPOINT
    ] = True

viewer.sync()

docs.title: 74. XML parse error
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_01_inspect.py --headless

MuJoCo normally reports the XML line containing the problem.

Common causes:

  • missing closing tag;
  • misspelled attribute;
  • wrong number of keyframe values;
  • invalid joint range;
  • invalid body reference.

docs.title: 75. Humanoid immediately falls
docs.text:
Check which exercise is running.

Exercises 1 and 2 should use the pelvis weld.

Exercises 3 and 4 should release the pelvis.

A released humanoid without whole-body balance control may eventually fall even when no push is applied.

docs.title: 76. Violent oscillation
docs.text:
Restore reasonable values:

<option timestep="0.002"
        integrator="implicitfast"/>

<joint damping="3"
       armature="0.02"/>

<position kp="140"/>

Very high gain and low damping can create oscillation and large contact forces.

docs.title: 77. Friction change has little visible effect
docs.text:
Ensure friction is changed for:

  • the floor;
  • the left foot;
  • the right foot.

Also increase the lateral force if the disturbance is too small.

docs.title: 78. Touch sensor always reads zero
docs.text:
Check:

  • whether the site overlaps the contact region;
  • whether the feet actually contact the floor;
  • whether the sensor name is correct;
  • whether the sensor address is read correctly;
  • whether the pelvis weld holds the feet slightly above the floor.

docs.title: 79. Short questions
docs.text:
1. What is the difference between MjModel and MjData?
  2. Why does a free joint use 7 position values and 6 velocity values?
  3. What is the purpose of mj_forward?
  4. What does data.ctrl contain?
  5. Why is a position actuator not equivalent to assigning qpos?
  6. What does a touch sensor measure?
  7. Why does low friction increase slipping?
  8. Why is projected centre of mass only a static approximation?
  9. How can an external force be applied to a body?
  10. Why must a fall criterion be defined by the experimenter?

docs.title: 80. Practical assessment
docs.text:
Ask students to:

  1. load the standing keyframe;
  2. command a crouching posture;
  3. release the pelvis weld;
  4. set friction to 0.2;
  5. apply a 25 N push for 0.2 s;
  6. report:
  7. whether the robot fell;
  8. maximum torso tilt;
  9. minimum pelvis height;
  10. left and right foot force;
  11. approximate foot displacement.

docs.title: Part XIII — Core conclusions
docs.text:
The workshop should end with these statements:

  1. A valid humanoid pose is not automatically dynamically stable.

  2. Joint commands create actuator forces; they do not directly replace the physical state.

  3. A floating base makes whole-body balance fundamentally different from fixed-base joint control.

  4. Contact includes geometry, normal forces, friction, and possible sliding.

  5. Simulation results depend on both the physical model and the experimental definition.

  6. A fall detector is an engineering criterion, not an automatic truth supplied by the simulator.

  7. The same principles apply to simplified humanoids and detailed models such as the Unitree G1.

Library / ADC Prep /mujoco_humanoid_workshop_tutorial.md

docs.title: 1. Workshop purpose
docs.text:
This workshop introduces the simulation concepts needed for humanoid robotics using MuJoCo.

The workshop focuses entirely on the simulator. It does not use:

  • machine learning;
  • reinforcement learning;
  • Gymnasium;
  • Stable-Baselines3;
  • ROS;
  • trajectory optimization.

The main topics are:

  • MJCF model structure;
  • floating-base humanoids;
  • generalized coordinates;
  • joint actuators;
  • scripted posture changes;
  • contact forces;
  • friction;
  • centre of mass;
  • external disturbances;
  • fall detection.

The workshop uses a simplified humanoid model first. A Unitree G1 model can be shown at the end as a more realistic example.

docs.title: 2. Learning outcomes
docs.text:
At the end of the workshop, students should be able to:

  1. load an MJCF humanoid model;
  2. identify bodies, joints, geoms, sites, actuators, sensors, and keyframes;
  3. explain the difference between MjModel and MjData;
  4. inspect qpos, qvel, and ctrl;
  5. reset a simulation using a keyframe;
  6. command position actuators;
  7. generate a smooth scripted posture;
  8. visualize and measure foot contact;
  9. modify friction parameters;
  10. apply an external force;
  11. define a simple fall criterion;
  12. explain why a valid humanoid pose is not necessarily dynamically stable.

docs.title: 3. Recommended workshop structure
docs.text: Time Activity 0–10 min Introduction and demonstration 10–30 min Exercise 1: Inspect the humanoid 30–55 min Exercise 2: Scripted posture control 55–65 min Discussion and short break 65–90 min Exercise 3: Contact, CoM, and friction 90–115 min Exercise 4: Push test and fall detection 115–120 min Unitree G1 comparison and conclusions

docs.title: 4. Recommended environment
docs.text:
Recommended system:

  • Ubuntu 22.04 or newer;
  • Python 3.10 or newer;
  • working OpenGL desktop environment;
  • one computer per student or student pair.

The workshop requires only:

mujoco
numpy

docs.title: 5. Extract the workshop package
docs.text:
Unzip the workshop archive:

unzip mujoco_humanoid_workshop_fixed.zip
cd mujoco_humanoid_workshop

The folder should contain:

mujoco_humanoid_workshop/
├── README.md
├── requirements.txt
├── common.py
├── exercise_01_inspect.py
├── exercise_02_pose.py
├── exercise_03_contacts.py
├── exercise_04_push_test.py
└── models/
    └── simple_humanoid.xml

docs.title: 6. Create a Python virtual environment
docs.text:
Create the environment:

python3 -m venv .venv

Activate it:

source .venv/bin/activate

Upgrade pip:

python -m pip install --upgrade pip

Install the requirements:

python -m pip install -r requirements.txt

Verify MuJoCo:

python -c "import mujoco; print(mujoco.__version__)"

docs.title: 7. Test the model without graphics
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_01_inspect.py --headless --duration 0.1

Expected final output:

Simulation completed at t=0.10 s

This confirms that:

  • MuJoCo is installed;
  • the XML model compiles;
  • the physics loop works.

docs.title: 8. Test the graphical viewer
docs.text:
Run:

python -m mujoco.viewer --mjcf=models/simple_humanoid.xml

A humanoid model should appear above the floor.

Close the viewer before continuing.

docs.title: 9. MjModel and MjData
docs.text:
A basic MuJoCo program begins with:

import mujoco

model = mujoco.MjModel.from_xml_path(
    "models/simple_humanoid.xml"
)

data = mujoco.MjData(model)

docs.title: MjModel
docs.text:
MjModel contains the compiled model definition.

Examples include:

  • body masses;
  • joint types;
  • actuator gains;
  • geometry dimensions;
  • friction coefficients;
  • sensor definitions;
  • simulation timestep.

The model is mostly constant during a simulation.

docs.title: MjData
docs.text:
MjData contains the changing simulation state.

Examples include:

  • joint positions;
  • joint velocities;
  • actuator commands;
  • body positions;
  • contact information;
  • sensor values;
  • simulation time.

docs.title: 10. The simulation loop
docs.text:
A simple loop is:

while simulation_is_running:
    data.ctrl[:] = control_command
    mujoco.mj_step(model, data)

mj_step:

  1. evaluates forces;
  2. solves contacts and constraints;
  3. calculates accelerations;
  4. integrates the state;
  5. advances simulation time.

The simulation timestep is stored in:

model.opt.timestep

For example:

<option timestep="0.002"
        gravity="0 0 -9.81"/>

A timestep of 0.002 seconds corresponds to:

[ f = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500\ \text{Hz} ]

docs.title: 11. Main MJCF sections
docs.text:
Open:

models/simple_humanoid.xml

The major sections are:

<mujoco>
    <option/>
    <default/>
    <asset/>
    <worldbody/>
    <equality/>
    <actuator/>
    <sensor/>
    <keyframe/>
</mujoco>

docs.title: 12. Bodies
docs.text:
A body represents a rigid coordinate frame in the kinematic tree.

Example:

<body name="pelvis" pos="0 0 0.92">

A child body is positioned relative to its parent.

Example:

<body name="left_shin" pos="0 0 -0.36">

This means the shin frame is positioned 0.36 m below its parent body.

The simplified hierarchy is:

pelvis
├── torso
│   ├── head
│   ├── left_arm
│   └── right_arm
├── left_thigh
│   └── left_shin
│       └── left_foot
└── right_thigh
    └── right_shin
        └── right_foot

docs.title: 13. Joints
docs.text:
A joint defines relative motion between a body and its parent.

Example hinge joint:

<joint name="left_knee"
       type="hinge"
       axis="0 1 0"
       range="0 2.1"/>

This joint:

  • rotates around the local (y)-axis;
  • has a minimum angle of 0 rad;
  • has a maximum angle of 2.1 rad.

Convert the maximum to degrees:

[ 2.1 \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 120.3^\circ ]

docs.title: 14. The floating base
docs.text:
The pelvis has a free joint:

<freejoint name="root"/>

A free joint allows:

  • translation in (x);
  • translation in (y);
  • translation in (z);
  • rotation around all three axes.

A free joint occupies:

  • 7 entries in qpos;
  • 6 entries in qvel.

docs.title: Position representation
docs.text:
The free joint position uses:

  • 3 Cartesian coordinates;
  • 4 quaternion coordinates.

Therefore:

[ n_{q,\mathrm{free}} = 3 + 4 = 7 ]

docs.title: Velocity representation
docs.text:
The free joint velocity uses:

  • 3 linear velocities;
  • 3 angular velocities.

Therefore:

[ n_{v,\mathrm{free}} = 3 + 3 = 6 ]

The teaching humanoid has:

  • one free joint;
  • twelve hinge joints.

Therefore:

[ n_q = 7 + 12 = 19 ]

[ n_v = 6 + 12 = 18 ]

docs.title: 15. Geoms
docs.text:
A geom can provide:

  • visual shape;
  • collision shape;
  • mass;
  • inertia;
  • contact properties.

Example:

<geom name="left_foot_geom"
      type="box"
      size="0.12 0.05 0.03"
      friction="0.9 0.005 0.0001"/>

A box size specifies half-dimensions.

Therefore, this foot has approximate full dimensions:

[ 0.24 \times 0.10 \times 0.06\ \text{m} ]

docs.title: 16. Sites
docs.text:
A site is a reference location or volume.

Sites are often used for:

  • sensors;
  • end-effector markers;
  • contact measurement regions;
  • visualization;
  • force application reference points.

Example:

<site name="left_foot_touch_site"
      type="box"
      size="0.12 0.05 0.015"/>

docs.title: 17. Actuators
docs.text:
The workshop uses position actuators.

Example:

<position name="left_knee_act"
          joint="left_knee"
          kp="140"
          ctrlrange="0 2.1"/>

The command is written to:

data.ctrl

For a position actuator, the control value acts as a desired joint position.

However, the joint is not teleported to that position. The actuator generates force based on the actuator model.

The resulting motion also depends on:

  • inertia;
  • gravity;
  • damping;
  • contact;
  • actuator gain;
  • constraints;
  • timestep.

docs.title: 18. Keyframes
docs.text:
Keyframes store predefined simulation states.

Example:

<keyframe>
    <key name="stand"
         qpos="..."
         ctrl="..."/>

    <key name="crouch"
         qpos="..."
         ctrl="..."/>
</keyframe>

Reset to a keyframe:

key_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_KEY,
    "stand",
)

mujoco.mj_resetDataKeyframe(
    model,
    data,
    key_id,
)

mujoco.mj_forward(model, data)

mj_forward recalculates derived quantities after manually changing the state.

docs.title: 19. Pelvis weld
docs.text:
The model includes an equality constraint:

<equality>
    <weld name="pelvis_weld"
          body1="pelvis"/>
</equality>

This weld can connect the pelvis to the world.

docs.title: Fixed-base mode
docs.text:
Used in Exercises 1 and 2.

The humanoid cannot fall.

This allows students to focus on:

  • model structure;
  • joint coordinates;
  • actuator commands;
  • scripted posture changes.

docs.title: Floating-base mode
docs.text:
Used in Exercises 3 and 4.

The weld is disabled from Python:

weld_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_EQUALITY,
    "pelvis_weld",
)

data.eq_active[weld_id] = 0

The humanoid can then:

  • translate;
  • rotate;
  • slip;
  • tip;
  • fall.

docs.title: Part IV — Exercise 1: Inspect the humanoid
20. Objective
docs.text: Understand how an MJCF description becomes an indexed numerical model.

docs.title: 21. Run the exercise
docs.text:
python exercise_01_inspect.py

The script prints:

  • body count;
  • joint count;
  • number of position coordinates;
  • number of velocity coordinates;
  • actuator count;
  • sensor count;
  • joint names;
  • joint ranges;
  • qpos addresses;
  • degree-of-freedom addresses;
  • actuator names;
  • sensor names.

docs.title: 22. Student task: inspect the model dimensions
docs.text:
Expected values are approximately:

Bodies:               12
Joints:               13
Position coordinates: 19
Velocity coordinates: 18
Actuators:            12
Sensors:              6
Equality constraints: 1

Ask:

  1. Why are there more bodies than actuators?
  2. Why is nq different from nv?
  3. Why does the head have no actuator?
  4. Which joint represents the floating base?

docs.title: 23. Student task: inspect joint names
docs.text:
A typical inspection loop is:

for joint_id in range(model.njnt):
    name = mujoco.mj_id2name(
        model,
        mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_JOINT,
        joint_id,
    )

    print(joint_id, name)

Students should identify:

root
left_hip_yaw
left_hip_roll
left_hip_pitch
left_knee
left_ankle_pitch
left_ankle_roll
right_hip_yaw
right_hip_roll
right_hip_pitch
right_knee
right_ankle_pitch
right_ankle_roll

docs.title: 24. Student task: modify a material
docs.text:
Find:

<material name="left_mat"
          rgba="0.25 0.65 0.35 1"/>

Change it to:

<material name="left_mat"
          rgba="0.8 0.2 0.8 1"/>

Restart:

python exercise_01_inspect.py

The model is compiled when loaded. XML changes do not affect a model that is already running.

docs.title: 25. Student task: modify one joint limit
docs.text:
Find:

<joint name="left_knee"
       range="0 2.1"/>

Change it temporarily:

<joint name="left_knee"
       range="0 1.2"/>

Restart the simulation and observe whether the crouching keyframe or commanded posture remains feasible.

Restore the original value afterward.

docs.title: 26. Exercise 1 discussion
docs.text:
Ask:

  • Which parts are visual only?
  • Which parts participate in collisions?
  • Why does the pelvis need a free joint?
  • Why are joint positions stored in an array?
  • Why are names converted to integer IDs?

docs.title: Main takeaway
docs.text: The XML is a human-readable model description. MuJoCo compiles it into numerical arrays used by the physics engine.

docs.title: Part V — Exercise 2: Scripted posture control
27. Objective
docs.text: Command the humanoid to move smoothly between standing and crouching poses.

docs.title: 28. Run the exercise
docs.text:
python exercise_02_pose.py

The robot should repeatedly move:

standing → crouching → standing

The pelvis is fixed during this exercise.

docs.title: 29. Actuator order
docs.text:
The control vector contains 12 values:

0  left hip yaw
1  left hip roll
2  left hip pitch
3  left knee
4  left ankle pitch
5  left ankle roll
6  right hip yaw
7  right hip roll
8  right hip pitch
9  right knee
10 right ankle pitch
11 right ankle roll

A crouching reference may be:

crouch = [
    0.0, 0.0, -0.45, 0.90, -0.45, 0.0,
    0.0, 0.0, -0.45, 0.90, -0.45, 0.0,
]

For each leg:

[ q_{\mathrm{hip}} + q_{\mathrm{knee}} + q_{\mathrm{ankle}} = -0.45 + 0.90 - 0.45 = 0 ]

This helps keep the foot approximately parallel to the floor.

docs.title: 30. Linear interpolation
docs.text:
The simplest interpolation is:

[ q_{\mathrm{ref}}(u) = q_{\mathrm{start}} + u \left( q_{\mathrm{goal}} - q_{\mathrm{start}} \right) ]

where:

[ 0 \leq u \leq 1 ]

Python:

q_ref = q_start + u * (q_goal - q_start)

This creates a constant desired velocity during the segment, but the desired velocity changes abruptly at the beginning and end.

docs.title: 31. Smoothstep interpolation
docs.text:
The workshop uses:

[ s(u)=3u^2-2u^3 ]

Then:

[ q_{\mathrm{ref}}(u) = q_{\mathrm{start}} + s(u) \left( q_{\mathrm{goal}} - q_{\mathrm{start}} \right) ]

Properties:

[ s(0)=0 ]

[ s(1)=1 ]

[ s'(0)=0 ]

[ s'(1)=0 ]

Python:

def smoothstep(value: float) -> float:
    value = float(np.clip(value, 0.0, 1.0))
    return 3.0 * value**2 - 2.0 * value**3

docs.title: 32. Student task: compare linear and smooth motion
docs.text:
Replace:

return 3.0 * value**2 - 2.0 * value**3

with:

return value

Run:

python exercise_02_pose.py

Compare:

  • movement onset;
  • movement termination;
  • visible oscillation;
  • tracking error;
  • apparent smoothness.

Restore the smoothstep function afterward.

docs.title: 33. Student task: modify actuator gain
docs.text:
Find the position actuator default:

<position kp="140"
          ctrllimited="true"/>

Test:

<position kp="40"
          ctrllimited="true"/>

Then:

<position kp="300"
          ctrllimited="true"/>

Record:

Gain Tracking speed Tracking error Oscillation 40 140 300

docs.title: Expected observations
docs.text:
Low gain:

  • slower response;
  • larger position error;
  • softer movement.

High gain:

  • faster response;
  • smaller static error;
  • possibly stronger oscillation;
  • larger forces;
  • greater numerical sensitivity.

docs.title: 34. Student task: modify joint damping
docs.text:
Find:

<joint damping="3"
       armature="0.02"
       limited="true"/>

Test:

damping="0.3"

Then:

damping="10"

Record the effect.

docs.title: Expected observations
docs.text:
Low damping:

  • more oscillation;
  • longer settling;
  • freer motion.

High damping:

  • reduced oscillation;
  • slower motion;
  • stronger resistance to rapid changes.

docs.title: 35. Exercise 2 discussion
docs.text:
Ask:

  1. Does data.ctrl directly set qpos?
  2. Why does the actual position lag behind the reference?
  3. Why can high gains cause instability?
  4. Why did we fix the pelvis?
  5. Would the same command work if link masses were doubled?

docs.title: Main takeaway
docs.text: An actuator command creates force. It does not directly replace the simulated joint state.

docs.title: Part VI — Discussion and short break
36. Suggested questions
docs.text:
1. Why did we fix the pelvis during the first two exercises?
  2. What changes when the floating base is released?
  3. Can a robot follow all joint commands and still fall?
  4. Is joint-space tracking identical to balance control?
  5. Why can an upright pose be dynamically unstable?

The central idea is:

Good joint tracking does not automatically produce whole-body stability.

docs.title: Part VII — Exercise 3: Contact, centre of mass, and friction
37. Objective
docs.text: Observe floor contact, foot forces, centre of mass, slipping, and load transfer.

docs.title: 38. Run the default experiment
docs.text:
python exercise_03_contacts.py --friction 0.9

The script:

  1. loads the standing keyframe;
  2. disables the pelvis weld;
  3. enables contact visualization;
  4. reads foot contact sensors;
  5. reads the centre-of-mass sensor;
  6. applies a small lateral force;
  7. measures foot displacement.

docs.title: 39. Viewer locking
docs.text:
When using the passive viewer, modify viewer state while holding the viewer lock:

with viewer.lock():
    viewer.opt.flags[
        mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTPOINT
    ] = True

    viewer.opt.flags[
        mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTFORCE
    ] = True

viewer.sync()

This avoids race conditions between the simulation thread and the viewer thread.

docs.title: 40. Foot touch sensors
docs.text:
The XML contains:

<touch name="left_foot_force"
       site="left_foot_touch_site"/>

<touch name="right_foot_force"
       site="right_foot_touch_site"/>

The touch sensor returns a non-negative scalar representing the summed normal contact force associated with contacts in the sensor site.

Read sensor data:

sensor_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_SENSOR,
    "left_foot_force",
)

start = model.sensor_adr[sensor_id]
dimension = model.sensor_dim[sensor_id]

left_force = data.sensordata[
    start:start + dimension
]

docs.title: 41. Centre-of-mass sensor
docs.text:
The XML contains:

<subtreecom name="humanoid_com"
            body="pelvis"/>

The sensor returns:

[ p_{\mathrm{CoM}} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{\mathrm{CoM}}\ y_{\mathrm{CoM}}\ z_{\mathrm{CoM}} \end{bmatrix} ]

For a basic balance interpretation, use the horizontal projection:

[ p_{\mathrm{CoM,xy}} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{\mathrm{CoM}}\ y_{\mathrm{CoM}} \end{bmatrix} ]

For static balance, the projected centre of mass should normally remain inside the support region formed by the contacting feet.

This is only an introductory approximation. Dynamic balance also depends on:

  • velocity;
  • angular momentum;
  • contact forces;
  • centre of pressure;
  • future motion.

docs.title: 42. Student task: compare left and right foot force
docs.text:
Before the disturbance:

left force ≈ right force

During a lateral disturbance:

left force ≠ right force

Ask:

  • Which foot becomes more loaded?
  • Does the foot with greater force necessarily move less?
  • Does force difference directly indicate body velocity?

The answer to the last question is no. Contact force and body velocity are related through dynamics, but they are not the same quantity.

docs.title: 43. Friction parameters
docs.text:
The floor geom contains:

friction="0.9 0.005 0.0001"

The three components correspond to:

  1. sliding friction;
  2. torsional friction;
  3. rolling friction.

For this workshop, the first value is the most important.

docs.title: 44. Student task: friction experiment
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_03_contacts.py --friction 0.9

Then:

python exercise_03_contacts.py --friction 0.3

Then:

python exercise_03_contacts.py --friction 0.05

Record:

Sliding friction Left-foot displacement Right-foot displacement Visible slipping 0.90 0.30 0.05

Expected trend:

  • high friction resists sliding;
  • low friction allows larger foot displacement;
  • very low friction may produce sliding before strong body tilt.

docs.title: 45. Student task: modify the lateral disturbance
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_03_contacts.py \
    --friction 0.3 \
    --lateral-force 10

Then:

python exercise_03_contacts.py \
    --friction 0.3 \
    --lateral-force 40

Observe:

  • load transfer;
  • foot sliding;
  • torso tilt;
  • contact loss;
  • whole-body translation.

docs.title: 46. Contact versus stability
docs.text:
A humanoid can have both feet touching the floor and still be unstable.

Possible situations include:

  • centre of mass leaving the support region;
  • insufficient friction;
  • excessive angular velocity;
  • one foot unloading;
  • contact occurring only at an edge;
  • feet sliding together.

docs.title: Main takeaway
docs.text: Contact is not simply “on” or “off.” It has geometry, forces, friction, and motion.

docs.title: Part VIII — Exercise 4: Push test and fall detection
47. Objective
docs.text: Apply a repeatable external disturbance and measure whether the humanoid falls.

docs.title: 48. Run the push test
docs.text:
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 30

The script applies a horizontal force to the torso.

docs.title: 49. External force array
docs.text:
MuJoCo provides:

data.xfrc_applied

Each body receives a six-component spatial force:

Fx Fy Fz Tx Ty Tz

Apply a force in the world (x)-direction:

torso_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_BODY,
    "torso",
)

data.xfrc_applied[torso_id, 0] = 30.0

Clear the force afterward:

data.xfrc_applied[torso_id, :] = 0.0

docs.title: 50. Push timing
docs.text:
The force is applied only during a defined interval:

if push_start <= data.time < push_start + push_duration:
    data.xfrc_applied[torso_id, 0] = push_force
else:
    data.xfrc_applied[torso_id, :] = 0.0

Typical settings:

push_start    = 0.50 s
push_duration = 0.15 s

docs.title: 51. Force and impulse
docs.text:
Force magnitude alone does not fully describe the disturbance.

Impulse is:

[ J = F \Delta t ]

For a 30 N force applied for 0.15 s:

[ J = 30 \cdot 0.15 = 4.5\ \mathrm{N\,s} ]

For 40 N:

[ J = 40 \cdot 0.15 = 6.0\ \mathrm{N\,s} ]

docs.title: 52. Student task: force sweep
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 20 --headless
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 25 --headless
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 30 --headless
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 35 --headless
python exercise_04_push_test.py --force 40 --headless

Record:

Force Impulse Fallen Maximum tilt Minimum pelvis height 20 N 3.00 N·s 25 N 3.75 N·s 30 N 4.50 N·s 35 N 5.25 N·s 40 N 6.00 N·s

docs.title: 53. Fall criterion based on pelvis height
docs.text:
Get the pelvis position:

pelvis_id = mujoco.mj_name2id(
    model,
    mujoco.mjtObj.mjOBJ_BODY,
    "pelvis",
)

pelvis_height = data.xpos[pelvis_id, 2]

Simple criterion:

pelvis_height < 0.55

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• simple;
  • easy to understand;
  • computationally inexpensive.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• a deep crouch may be classified as a fall;
  • a leaning robot may remain above the threshold;
  • body orientation is ignored.

docs.title: 54. Fall criterion based on torso orientation
docs.text:
The body rotation matrix is stored in:

data.xmat[torso_id]

Reshape it:

rotation = data.xmat[torso_id].reshape(3, 3)

The local torso (z)-axis expressed in world coordinates is the third column.

For the workshop model:

torso_up_z = rotation[2, 2]

For an upright torso:

[ z_{\mathrm{up}} \approx 1 ]

For a horizontal torso:

[ z_{\mathrm{up}} \approx 0 ]

A threshold of:

torso_up_z < 0.65

corresponds approximately to:

[ \theta > \cos^{-1}(0.65) \approx 49.5^\circ ]

docs.title: 55. Combined fall criterion
docs.text:
Use:

fallen = (
    pelvis_height < 0.55
    or torso_up_z < 0.65
)

This combines:

  • body height;
  • body orientation.

It is more robust than either condition alone.

docs.title: 56. Student task: change the fall threshold
docs.text:
Try:

torso_up_z < 0.85

This corresponds to:

[ \theta > \cos^{-1}(0.85) \approx 31.8^\circ ]

Then try:

torso_up_z < 0.30

This corresponds to:

[ \theta > \cos^{-1}(0.30) \approx 72.5^\circ ]

Discuss:

  • false positives;
  • false negatives;
  • how the experimental definition changes the reported result.

docs.title: 57. Student task: compare equal impulses
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_04_push_test.py \
    --force 40 \
    --push-duration 0.10 \
    --headless

Impulse:

[ J = 40 \cdot 0.10 = 4.0\ \mathrm{N\,s} ]

Then:

python exercise_04_push_test.py \
    --force 20 \
    --push-duration 0.20 \
    --headless

Impulse:

[ J = 20 \cdot 0.20 = 4.0\ \mathrm{N\,s} ]

Ask:

Will the resulting motion be identical?

Not necessarily.

Even with equal impulse:

  • the force magnitude differs;
  • the body moves during the push;
  • contact conditions change;
  • joint velocities change;
  • the model is nonlinear.

docs.title: 58. Student task: compare friction during the push
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_04_push_test.py \
    --force 30 \
    --friction 0.9 \
    --headless

Then:

python exercise_04_push_test.py \
    --force 30 \
    --friction 0.1 \
    --headless

Determine whether failure occurs primarily through:

  • sliding;
  • tipping;
  • contact loss;
  • a combination of these.

docs.title: 59. Final engineering challenge
docs.text:
Find the largest horizontal push that the humanoid can survive for five seconds.

Students may modify:

  • stance width;
  • knee angle;
  • actuator gain;
  • joint damping;
  • foot friction.

Students may not modify:

  • gravity;
  • body masses;
  • push duration;
  • fall criterion.

Suggested table:

Configuration Maximum force Fell? Maximum tilt Minimum pelvis height Default Wider stance Bent knees Higher damping Final design

docs.title: 60. Purpose
docs.text:
The G1 demonstration shows that the same concepts transfer to a detailed commercial humanoid model.

The detailed model still contains:

  • bodies;
  • joints;
  • geoms;
  • actuators;
  • sensors;
  • contacts;
  • floating-base coordinates.

docs.title: 61. Obtain MuJoCo Menagerie
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone https://github.com/google-deepmind/mujoco_menagerie.git

Open the G1 model:

python -m mujoco.viewer \
    --mjcf mujoco_menagerie/unitree_g1/scene.xml

docs.title: 62. Student observation tasks
docs.text:
Ask students to identify:

  1. pelvis or trunk root;
  2. floating base;
  3. hip joints;
  4. knee joints;
  5. ankle joints;
  6. foot collision geometry;
  7. actuator definitions;
  8. keyframes;
  9. joint limits;
  10. differences from the simplified model.

docs.title: 63. Discussion
docs.text:
Ask:

  • Why is the G1 XML much longer?
  • Which details are physical?
  • Which details are visual?
  • Which concepts are unchanged?
  • Why is a simplified model useful for learning?
  • Why is a detailed model useful for validation?

docs.title: Main takeaway
docs.text: A realistic humanoid is more complex, but it is built from the same simulator concepts.

docs.title: 64. Opening explanation
docs.text: In this workshop, we will not design an intelligent controller. Our goal is to understand what a humanoid simulator actually represents. We will inspect the model, command the joints, release the floating base, observe contact, change friction, and finally apply a controlled push.

docs.title: 65. Before Exercise 1
docs.text: The XML file is the human-readable description. MuJoCo compiles it into indexed arrays. The simulator does not repeatedly search for a joint by name during every dynamics calculation.

docs.title: 66. Before Exercise 2
docs.text: A position actuator does not teleport a joint. It creates force according to the difference between the command and the simulated state. The motion still depends on physics.

docs.title: 67. Before Exercise 3
docs.text: Until now, the pelvis was fixed. This separated joint actuation from whole-body balance. We will now release the root and let the robot interact freely with the floor.

docs.title: 68. Before Exercise 4
docs.text: A push test is only useful if it is repeatable. We must define the force, duration, application point, friction, initial state, and fall criterion.

docs.title: 69. Final conclusion
docs.text: A valid pose is not automatically stable. A joint controller does not directly control the floating base. Humanoid simulation is the interaction of model structure, actuation, contacts, and measurable evaluation criteria.

docs.title: 70. MuJoCo is not installed
docs.text:
Error:

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'mujoco'

Activate the environment:

source .venv/bin/activate

Install:

python -m pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 71. Test without graphics
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_01_inspect.py --headless --duration 0.1

If this works, the model and physics engine are functioning.

docs.title: 72. Viewer segmentation fault
docs.text:
First test the standalone viewer:

python -m mujoco.viewer \
    --mjcf=models/simple_humanoid.xml

If the standalone viewer also crashes, the issue is likely related to:

  • OpenGL;
  • GLFW;
  • graphics drivers;
  • remote desktop;
  • WSL graphics;
  • mixed Conda and pip libraries.

Use a clean virtual environment.

Avoid mixing:

  • Conda OpenGL libraries;
  • system MuJoCo installations;
  • pip MuJoCo installations.

Run with fault reporting:

python -X faulthandler exercise_01_inspect.py

docs.title: 73. Viewer-state changes
docs.text:
When using:

mujoco.viewer.launch_passive(...)

protect viewer changes:

with viewer.lock():
    viewer.opt.flags[
        mujoco.mjtVisFlag.mjVIS_CONTACTPOINT
    ] = True

viewer.sync()

docs.title: 74. XML parse error
docs.text:
Run:

python exercise_01_inspect.py --headless

MuJoCo normally reports the XML line containing the problem.

Common causes:

  • missing closing tag;
  • misspelled attribute;
  • wrong number of keyframe values;
  • invalid joint range;
  • invalid body reference.

docs.title: 75. Humanoid immediately falls
docs.text:
Check which exercise is running.

Exercises 1 and 2 should use the pelvis weld.

Exercises 3 and 4 should release the pelvis.

A released humanoid without whole-body balance control may eventually fall even when no push is applied.

docs.title: 76. Violent oscillation
docs.text:
Restore reasonable values:

<option timestep="0.002"
        integrator="implicitfast"/>

<joint damping="3"
       armature="0.02"/>

<position kp="140"/>

Very high gain and low damping can create oscillation and large contact forces.

docs.title: 77. Friction change has little visible effect
docs.text:
Ensure friction is changed for:

  • the floor;
  • the left foot;
  • the right foot.

Also increase the lateral force if the disturbance is too small.

docs.title: 78. Touch sensor always reads zero
docs.text:
Check:

  • whether the site overlaps the contact region;
  • whether the feet actually contact the floor;
  • whether the sensor name is correct;
  • whether the sensor address is read correctly;
  • whether the pelvis weld holds the feet slightly above the floor.

docs.title: 79. Short questions
docs.text:
1. What is the difference between MjModel and MjData?
  2. Why does a free joint use 7 position values and 6 velocity values?
  3. What is the purpose of mj_forward?
  4. What does data.ctrl contain?
  5. Why is a position actuator not equivalent to assigning qpos?
  6. What does a touch sensor measure?
  7. Why does low friction increase slipping?
  8. Why is projected centre of mass only a static approximation?
  9. How can an external force be applied to a body?
  10. Why must a fall criterion be defined by the experimenter?

docs.title: 80. Practical assessment
docs.text:
Ask students to:

  1. load the standing keyframe;
  2. command a crouching posture;
  3. release the pelvis weld;
  4. set friction to 0.2;
  5. apply a 25 N push for 0.2 s;
  6. report:
  7. whether the robot fell;
  8. maximum torso tilt;
  9. minimum pelvis height;
  10. left and right foot force;
  11. approximate foot displacement.

docs.title: Part XIII — Core conclusions
docs.text:
The workshop should end with these statements:

  1. A valid humanoid pose is not automatically dynamically stable.

  2. Joint commands create actuator forces; they do not directly replace the physical state.

  3. A floating base makes whole-body balance fundamentally different from fixed-base joint control.

  4. Contact includes geometry, normal forces, friction, and possible sliding.

  5. Simulation results depend on both the physical model and the experimental definition.

  6. A fall detector is an engineering criterion, not an automatic truth supplied by the simulator.

  7. The same principles apply to simplified humanoids and detailed models such as the Unitree G1.

docs.title: Fundamentals
docs.text: This section will contain basic concepts required for humanoid robotics, including coordinate frames, joints, kinematics, dynamics, and control basics.

docs.title: Reinforcement Learning
docs.text:
Recommended learning path:

  1. simplified 3-link arm throwing,
  2. scripted ball release,
  3. learned ball release,
  4. fixed-body Unitree G1 arm throwing,
  5. random target positions,
  6. gradually unlocked humanoid joints.

docs.title: Workspace Setup
docs.text:
This section explains how to prepare the development environment used in the humanoid robotics examples.

The examples are mainly intended for Linux-based systems. The recommended setup is Ubuntu 22.04 LTS or a similar recent Linux distribution.

The first reinforcement learning examples use MuJoCo, Gymnasium, and Stable-Baselines3. The development path starts with a simplified 3-link throwing arm and then moves toward a full Unitree G1 humanoid model where only one arm is controlled and the rest of the robot is kept fixed.

docs.title: 1. System requirements
docs.text:
Recommended operating system:

  • Ubuntu 22.04 LTS
  • Python 3.10 or newer
  • Git
  • A working terminal environment
  • A dedicated Python virtual environment

Recommended hardware:

  • A modern laptop or desktop computer
  • At least 8 GB RAM
  • Intel Core i5 or better
  • A dedicated GPU is useful for larger learning tasks, but it is not required for the first examples

The first examples are designed to work on CPU-only systems. For example, an Intel Core i5 platform with Intel Iris Xe graphics is sufficient for the simplified 3-link arm and fixed-body humanoid arm throwing experiments.

docs.title: 2. Install basic system packages
docs.text:
Update the package list:

sudo apt update

Install the basic development tools:

sudo apt install -y git unzip python3 python3-pip python3-venv build-essential

Install packages commonly needed by MuJoCo and the viewer:

sudo apt install -y libgl1 libglfw3 libglew-dev libosmesa6-dev mesa-utils patchelf

Check the installed versions:

python3 --version
pip3 --version
git --version

docs.title: 3. Clone and prepare the repository
docs.text:
Create a workspace folder:

mkdir -p ~/humanoid_ws
cd ~/humanoid_ws

Clone the repository:

git clone https://github.com/robotlabor/humanoid-robot-fundamentals.git
cd humanoid-robot-fundamentals

Create and activate a Python virtual environment:

python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

Upgrade the Python packaging tools:

pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel

Install the required Python packages:

pip install -r requirements.txt

Check the active Python environment:

which python
which pip

The paths should contain .venv.

Example:

/home/user/humanoid_ws/humanoid-robot-fundamentals/.venv/bin/python
/home/user/humanoid_ws/humanoid-robot-fundamentals/.venv/bin/pip

docs.title: 4. Test the setup
docs.text:
Run the basic Python test:

python3 scripts/test_python_setup.py

Run the basic MuJoCo simulation test:

python3 scripts/test_mujoco_setup.py

Check that MuJoCo can be imported:

python3 -c "import mujoco; print(mujoco.__version__)"

To test graphical rendering, run the random 3-link viewer:

python3 scripts/random_policy_viewer.py

If a MuJoCo window appears with a simple arm, ball, and target, the graphical viewer is working.

docs.title: 5. Check the starter repository structure
docs.text:
After installing the starter files, the repository should contain the main folders for assets, environments, scripts, documentation, logs, and policies.

From the workspace folder, inspect the structure with:

cd ~/humanoid_ws
tree humanoid-robot-fundamentals

A typical structure after running the 3-link example may look similar to:

humanoid-robot-fundamentals
├── assets
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── three_link_throw.xml
│   └── unitree_g1_throw
│       └── README.md
├── docs
│   ├── computer_simulation
│   │   └── index.md
│   ├── fundamentals
│   │   └── index.md
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── reinforcement_learning
│   │   └── index.md
│   └── workspace_setup
│       └── index.md
├── envs
│   ├── g1_fixed_body_throw_env.py
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── three_link_throw_env.py
├── external
├── logs
├── mkdocs.yml
├── policies
├── README.md
├── requirements.txt
└── scripts
    ├── create_g1_throw_scene.py
    ├── evaluate_g1_throw.py
    ├── evaluate_throw.py
    ├── inspect_g1_bodies.py
    ├── inspect_g1.py
    ├── play_g1_throw.py
    ├── play_throw.py
    ├── random_g1_throw_viewer.py
    ├── random_policy_viewer.py
    ├── test_mujoco_setup.py
    ├── test_python_setup.py
    ├── train_g1_throw.py
    ├── train_throw.py
    └── view_g1.py

The folder:

assets/unitree_g1_throw/

is only a placeholder at first. It will later contain the generated G1 throwing scene:

assets/unitree_g1_throw/scene_throw.xml

The folder:

assets/unitree_g1/

is not included initially. It is created only after copying the Unitree G1 model from MuJoCo Menagerie.

Therefore, if this folder is missing at this stage, that is normal:

assets/unitree_g1/

It will be created later with:

mkdir -p assets/unitree_g1
cp -r external/mujoco_menagerie/unitree_g1/* assets/unitree_g1/

After training examples, additional generated files may appear, such as:

logs/
policies/three_link_throw_ppo.zip
envs/__pycache__/

These are runtime outputs and should normally not be committed to Git.

If needed, clean generated files with:

rm -rf logs
rm -rf envs/__pycache__

The repository .gitignore should exclude generated folders such as:

.venv/
__pycache__/
logs/
external/
policies/*.zip

docs.title: Part A — Simplified 3-link arm throwing
docs.text:
The first reinforcement learning task uses a planar 3-link arm that throws a ball toward a fixed target.

This verifies:

  • MuJoCo model loading,
  • Gymnasium environment creation,
  • PPO training,
  • policy saving,
  • policy playback,
  • ball weld and release logic,
  • reward debugging.

The simplified throwing scene is stored in:

assets/three_link_throw.xml

The corresponding Gymnasium environment is:

envs/three_link_throw_env.py

docs.title: 6. Run the random 3-link viewer
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/random_policy_viewer.py

Expected behavior:

3-link arm appears
ball appears
target appears
arm moves
ball releases after the scripted release time

This step does not use reinforcement learning yet. It only verifies that the scene, environment, viewer, and release logic are working.

docs.title: 7. Train the 3-link throwing policy
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/train_throw.py

Training runs in the terminal and usually does not open a simulator window.

Expected saved policy:

policies/three_link_throw_ppo.zip

If you want to stop training early, press:

Ctrl+C

The training script is designed to save the current policy when interrupted.

docs.title: 8. Play the trained 3-link policy
docs.text:
After training, run:

python3 scripts/play_throw.py

Expected behavior:

The MuJoCo viewer opens
The trained arm motion is shown
The ball releases and moves toward the target

docs.title: 9. Evaluate the 3-link policy
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/evaluate_throw.py

The script reports basic numerical results, such as the mean best distance to the target and hit rates.

A useful first success criterion is:

Mean best distance below 0.5 m

A better result is:

Hit rate below 30 cm above 50%

docs.title: Part B — Unitree G1 fixed-body throwing
docs.text:
After the 3-link task works, the next step is to use the full Unitree G1 humanoid model while keeping the RL problem simple.

The goal is:

Full Unitree G1 model visible in MuJoCo
Only one arm is controlled by RL
All other joints are held fixed at nominal values
Ball is attached to the hand
Ball is released at a scripted time
Target is fixed

This is not yet full-body humanoid throwing. It is an intermediate step between the simple 3-link arm and a full humanoid controller.

docs.title: 10. Fetch the Unitree G1 model
docs.text:
The starter repository contains the simulation scripts and reinforcement learning environments, but it does not include the Unitree G1 model assets.

The Unitree G1 MuJoCo model should be copied from MuJoCo Menagerie into:

assets/unitree_g1/

From the repository root, run:

cd ~/humanoid_ws/humanoid-robot-fundamentals
source .venv/bin/activate

Clone MuJoCo Menagerie:

mkdir -p external
git clone https://github.com/google-deepmind/mujoco_menagerie.git external/mujoco_menagerie

Copy the Unitree G1 model into the local assets folder:

mkdir -p assets/unitree_g1
cp -r external/mujoco_menagerie/unitree_g1/* assets/unitree_g1/

Check the copied files:

ls assets/unitree_g1

Typical files include:

g1.xml
scene.xml
scene_mjx.xml
assets/

The most important file for the next step is:

assets/unitree_g1/scene.xml

If scene.xml is missing, scripts/view_g1.py will stop with an error such as:

FileNotFoundError: Copy Unitree G1 assets to assets/unitree_g1 first.

If you already cloned MuJoCo Menagerie earlier and the folder external/mujoco_menagerie already exists, you do not need to clone it again. Just copy the G1 assets:

mkdir -p assets/unitree_g1
cp -r external/mujoco_menagerie/unitree_g1/* assets/unitree_g1/

docs.title: 11. View the Unitree G1 model
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/view_g1.py

If the full G1 humanoid appears in the MuJoCo viewer, the model assets are loaded correctly.

If you get this error:

FileNotFoundError: Copy Unitree G1 assets to assets/unitree_g1 first.

go back to the previous step and make sure that assets/unitree_g1/scene.xml exists:

ls assets/unitree_g1/scene.xml

docs.title: 12. Inspect G1 joint and actuator names
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/inspect_g1.py

This prints all joint and actuator names in the model.

Look for the right arm joints. They may have names similar to:

right_shoulder_pitch_joint
right_shoulder_roll_joint
right_shoulder_yaw_joint
right_elbow_joint
right_wrist_roll_joint
right_wrist_pitch_joint
right_wrist_yaw_joint

The exact names depend on the copied G1 model version.

docs.title: 13. Inspect G1 body names
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/inspect_g1_bodies.py

This prints candidate body names containing words such as:

right
wrist
hand

You need one of these body names to attach the ball to the right hand or wrist.

Example output may include a body such as:

right_wrist_yaw_link

Use the body name that best corresponds to the right hand or wrist.

docs.title: 14. Create the G1 throwing scene
docs.text:
Generate the throwing scene using the selected hand or wrist body:

python3 scripts/create_g1_throw_scene.py --hand-body RIGHT_HAND_OR_WRIST_BODY_NAME

Replace:

RIGHT_HAND_OR_WRIST_BODY_NAME

with the body name printed by inspect_g1_bodies.py.

Example:

python3 scripts/create_g1_throw_scene.py --hand-body right_wrist_yaw_link

This creates:

assets/unitree_g1_throw/scene_throw.xml

The generated scene contains:

Full Unitree G1 model
throw_ball
throw_target
hold_throw_ball weld constraint

Test the generated XML:

python3 -c "import mujoco; mujoco.MjModel.from_xml_path('assets/unitree_g1_throw/scene_throw.xml'); print('G1 throw XML OK')"

Expected output:

G1 throw XML OK

docs.title: 15. Run the random G1 throwing viewer
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/random_g1_throw_viewer.py

Expected behavior:

Full G1 appears
Only the selected arm moves
The ball starts near the hand
The ball is released after the scripted release time
The target is visible
The body and legs remain mostly fixed

This step is important before training. If the ball is not attached to the hand or the wrong body moves, fix the scene or joint mapping before continuing.

docs.title: 16. Train the G1 fixed-body throwing policy
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/train_g1_throw.py

This script trains headlessly. It does not normally open a simulator window.

Expected saved policy:

policies/g1_fixed_body_throw_ppo.zip

The first version uses:

scripted release
fixed target
right arm actions only
all other joints held at nominal values

docs.title: 17. Play the trained G1 policy
docs.text:
After training, run:

python3 scripts/play_g1_throw.py

This should open the MuJoCo viewer and show the trained policy.

Check:

Does the full G1 load correctly?
Is the ball attached to the hand at the beginning?
Does only the selected arm move?
Does the ball release?
Does the ball fly toward the target?
Do the legs and body remain mostly fixed?
Does the simulation remain stable?

docs.title: 18. Evaluate the trained G1 policy
docs.text:
Run:

python3 scripts/evaluate_g1_throw.py

Suggested first success criterion:

Mean best distance < 0.8 m

Good result:

Hit rate below 50 cm > 50%

Very good result:

Hit rate below 30 cm > 50%

Save a good policy before overwriting it:

cp policies/g1_fixed_body_throw_ppo.zip policies/g1_fixed_body_throw_scripted_release_good.zip

docs.title: Controlled fixed joints versus physically locked joints
docs.text: There are two ways to interpret “fixed joints.”

docs.title: Controlled fixed joints
docs.text:
This is the recommended first approach.

All joints still exist.
The full humanoid remains in the simulation.
Non-arm joints are commanded to stay at nominal positions.
Only the throwing arm receives RL actions.

This is easier, more flexible, and usually sufficient for the first humanoid throwing experiment.

docs.title: Physically locked joints
docs.text:
This means the joints cannot move at all. This requires more MJCF editing, such as:

removing joints
removing the floating base
adding equality constraints
using very tight joint ranges
using very high stiffness and damping

For the first G1 throwing task, use controlled fixed joints. If the body falls or moves too much, the floating base or torso can be fixed later.

docs.title: Scripted release versus learned release
docs.text:
The first throwing examples use a scripted ball release.

This means:

The ball is welded to the hand at the beginning.
At a predefined time, the weld is disabled.
The ball then flies freely.
